perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Compliance with Local Laws and Regulations The Manufacturer covenants that it is and will remain for the term of this agreement in compliance with any and all Local Laws and Regulations. This includes without limitation laws relating to business practice, workplace relations, safety and taxation.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Compliance with Local Laws and Regulations The Manufacturer covenants that it will make reasonable efforts to comply with Local Laws and Regulations relating to business practice, workplace relations, safety and taxation.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the obligation to 'remain in compliance' to 'make reasonable efforts to comply', the contract introduces ambiguity and potentially allows the manufacturer to skirt mandatory legal requirements. This is especially problematic regarding workplace safety.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970
perturbation.law_explanation: The Occupational Safety and Health Act mandates that employers 'shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees.' By only requiring 'reasonable efforts' to comply, the modified contract language contradicts this strict legal duty to provide a safe workplace.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 15 - OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH Sec. 654 - Duties of employers and employees From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §654. Duties of employers and employees (a) Each employer— (1) shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees; (2) shall comply with occupational safety and health standards promulgated under this chapter. (b) Each employee shall comply with occupational safety and health standards and all rules, regulations, and orders issued pursuant to this chapter which are applicable to his own actions and conduct. (Pub. L. 91–596, §5, Dec. 29, 1970, 84 Stat. 1593.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language, which changes the obligation to 'make reasonable efforts to comply' with local laws, directly contradicts the legal duty imposed by the Occupational Safety and Health Act (OSHA). Snippet 1, specifically 29 U.S.C. §654(a)(1), mandates that employers 'shall furnish' a workplace free from recognized hazards. The original contract reflected this strict obligation. The modified language weakens this requirement by only requiring reasonable efforts, which could potentially excuse a failure to maintain a safe workplace if the employer argues they made reasonable efforts, even if those efforts were insufficient. This directly undermines the 'shall furnish' mandate, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Rejection of defective products The Customer shall inspect all Products promptly upon receipt thereof and may reject any defective Product, provided that the Customer shall within seven (7) days after receipt of such alleged defective Product, notify the Manufacturer of its rejection and either: (i) request to destroy in field for credit of the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs (with approval), or (ii) request a Return Material Authorization ("RMA") number and within seven (7) days of receipt of the RMA number from the Manufacturer return such rejected Product to the Manufacturer. Products not rejected within the foregoing time periods shall be deemed accepted by the Customer. In the event that the Manufacturer determines that the returned Product is defective and properly rejected by the Customer, the Manufacturer shall credit to the Customer the value of the defective product and the associated shipping costs.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 Rejection of defective products The Customer shall inspect all Products promptly upon receipt thereof and may reject any defective Product, provided that the Customer shall within seven (7) days after receipt of such alleged defective Product, notify the Manufacturer of its rejection. Products not rejected within the foregoing time periods shall be deemed accepted by the Customer.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the options for resolution (destruction in the field or RMA) after rejection severely limits the customer's recourse for defective products. This is particularly problematic if the products are perishable or expensive to ship back.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-608. Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-608 grants buyers the right to revoke acceptance of goods that do not conform to the contract if the non-conformity substantially impairs the value of the goods and the buyer accepted the goods without discovery of the non-conformity or with the assumption that it would be cured. By severely restricting the remedies available upon rejection, the modified contract language undermines this right to revocation and return of non-conforming goods.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-608. Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-608. Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part. (1) The buyer may revoke his acceptance of a lot or commercial unit whose non-conformity substantially impairs its value to him if he has accepted it (a) on the reasonable assumption that its non-conformity would be cured and it has not been seasonably cured; or (b) without discovery of such non-conformity if his acceptance was reasonably induced either by the difficulty of discovery before acceptance or by the seller's assurances. (2) Revocation of acceptance must occur within a reasonable time after the buyer discovers or should have discovered the ground for it and before any substantial change in condition of the goods which is not caused by their own defects. It is not effective until the buyer notifies the seller of it. (3) A buyer who so revokes has the same rights and duties with regard to the goods involved as if he had rejected them. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the options for resolving issues with defective products, which weakens the buyer's rights to remedies for non-conforming goods. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 2-608, states that a buyer who revokes acceptance has the same rights and duties as if they had rejected the goods. By removing the options of 'destroy in field for credit' or 'request a Return Material Authorization,' the contract limits the buyer's ability to exercise these rights after rejection, contradicting the rights provided under the UCC. The omission removes essential information, leading to a legal loophole where the consumer's protection is not compliant with legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The Manufacturing Companies utilised by the Agent to manufacture the products listed in Schedule A of this agreement shall maintain throughout the term of this agreement product liability insurance issued by a reputable insurance company under standard terms and conditions in the industry to cover the liability of the Customer and to indemnity the Customer from any costs, expenses, loss or damages resulting from any act, neglect or default of the company.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The Manufacturing Companies utilised by the Agent to manufacture the products listed in Schedule A of this agreement may maintain product liability insurance.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain' to 'may maintain' removes the *requirement* for the manufacturer to have product liability insurance that covers the customer. This leaves the customer exposed to significant liability should a defect cause harm. Since the territory is global, the absence of this insurance is a major liability concern.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Warranty of Merchantability, Uniform Commercial Code § 2-314
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Commercial Code's implied warranty of merchantability requires that goods be fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. While not explicitly requiring insurance, the absence of product liability insurance leaves the customer with no effective recourse if a product breaches this warranty and causes harm. By removing the requirement for the manufacturer to maintain insurance, the modified text increases the customer's risk related to product liability, effectively contradicting the protection offered by implied warranty laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that manufacturing companies maintain product liability insurance, while the changed contract makes it optional ('may maintain'). This omission directly contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that goods must be 'fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used.' Without the mandatory insurance, the customer's recourse is severely limited if a product is defective and causes harm, thus undermining the protection offered by the implied warranty. The modification removes a crucial layer of protection, making the contract less compliant with standard merchantability principles.
